text: No problem, Ashley.

You, um, joined me into the room as soon as I got distracted and bored and started dicking around with the video things, and I literally just clicked it, and as soon as I clicked it, it disappeared and you joined.

I love it, it's fabulous. Good morning, Amy. No, I don't want to see you bending over the toilet, Josh. That is true. Good to have you here, Caleb. I forget what you look like. I'll rescale it. Go back to the moon.

I like the moon. Morning, Dylan, and good morning, Tom. Tom would be on the tools as well. Oh, hang on, we don't want to hear Josh bending over the toilet either. Sorry, Josh. Good. All right, um, there's a couple more that are due to come, so we might just get started. That's okay, Tom, no stress.

And you're fine, Josh, you're all good, everyone. It's not a problem. Uh, you're going to do the heavy lifting because I only have a couple of actual questions today. I did get a bunch of submissions, so I appreciate the submissions.

Um, we're going to go back to something that we used to do that we stopped doing when, uh, we had Stuart join because it was too much for the interpreters to manage. But he's not here and he hasn't been here for a long time, so we're going to go back to the way we used to do things and get excited.
Amy obviously didn't like that. She's decided I don't want to play anymore.

Um, but to get us started today, we're going to take turns to unmute those who can, I will call on you. If you can't unmute, just don't. And I'd like you to tell me two things: one win and one thing that didn't go to plan. Can be personal or professional. I'd prefer work, but I'm okay if it's personal. Morning, Brendan. Um, so we're going to start with, uh, Rhys because he's deep in thought. Rhys, let me know one win and one something that didn't quite go to plan?
Sure. Well, the win is I finally fulfilled my life dream of making it to the moon. I've always really wanted to do that, so I was really excited to achieve that.

No, my, my win is I went for a trip to Melbourne the other week to go look at a job for a new client, and they haven't quite said that I've got the job, but they've pretty much said I've got the job. Fabulous.
And that's exciting because A, it's in a whole different state, B, I've never met them before, and C, it's a moderate-sized job, which is what I'm trying to get more into. Excellent. Well done.

What didn't go according to plan? A couple of weeks ago, we also did a new job for a new client, and after we were on site for, I think it was 3 or 4 days, he then announced, oh no, I've decided your bill's too high, so I'm not paying you. So we got to go on site and do 3 or 4 days worth of work, and I've submitted the invoice, and I have a feeling like I'm going to have a fight ahead of me.
So, gosh, Rhys, we haven't spoken about that one.
No, because, uh, had we spoken about it at the time, I wouldn't have wanted to say words in front of polite company that I would have probably said. So, wow.
Okay, that's not great. We might have to work on that. Thank you very much for sharing. It's nice to know that all of us have this problem at times. Um, thank you, Rhys. Thanks, man.

I'm pleased you're on the moon. Um, Amy Jones keeps falling on and off, so we'll come back to her. Brendan, are you able to— um, well, hang on, I might be able to unmute you. So yeah, there you go. Could you tell us one week— uh, sorry, one win from this week and something that didn't quite go to plan?
Oh, it's quite a lot that didn't go to plan, but, um, a win— oh, We've started 2 new jobs this week under our new entity, which is good. Wonderful. To me, that's a win. Yes.
And yeah, we're starting on the structural steel posts, which are pretty cool looking posts and like a ship sail. Oh wow.
We've been building them while it's been pissing down with rain. So that was a win as well. We had work while it was pouring. Excellent.
Um, things that haven't gone to plan.
Uh, we got an invoice this morning from an electrician on a reno we finished 2 weeks ago with, uh, $11,000 worth of extras that I was not consulted about.
Oh, who authorized the extras, Brendan? The homeowner?
Well, I'm assuming so. I'm— me and Kristen are going to delve into that one a little bit later today when I get back to the office. Oh my goodness.
But that'll hurt me significantly if the owners haven't approved it, and if they thought that it was already included, I don't know.
Oh, that's okay, we're gonna— we will navigate this. We've had a bit of practice this, Brendan. I think we're going to be okay.
Yeah, so that was probably the upset of the week so far. Yeah, that's shocking.
Yeah, other than that, everything was looking okay. So I, my mental capacity was brilliant. And then this morning it just went to shit. So kicking the guts.
Yeah. Yeah. Hopefully we can, hopefully we can work it out.

Okay. I'm sure that we can. I feel confident. Thank you for sharing. Can we not continue on with the invoice theme, please, people? Unless it's good news. Let's find a new theme. I don't like that one. Amy Jones, before your internet does what it does and you drop off again, would you like to share for us a win for this week and something that didn't go to plan, please?

Um, we've got that big— look, we won that big, uh, $20,000 job which turned into a $30,000 job, so that's a win. Um, and then day one of that, the subcontractor who was doing the underscrubbing um, his machine broke down on day one. Oh, so he only got in half a day instead of 4, 4 days.
So, um, has— he hired a different machine the next day. So yeah, he stopped the clock once he wasn't actually working anymore, and then he fixed the problem.
And yeah, and then it rained, and one of our staff decided that he didn't want to work in the rain after driving 40 minutes to the work site in our vehicle, using our fuel, and then home.
And yeah, so then obviously we couldn't— we also couldn't use the cherry picker when it was raining, so that pushed everything out a little bit. But everything is still okay.
Good, pleased to hear it. Maybe I don't really want to know what's going wrong for everyone because some of these things are enormous. Thank you for sharing, Amy Jones.
I'm going to Bali on Monday and I don't give a shit.

Good work. I think if nothing else, sharing what's gone wrong for us all makes the rest of us feel human. Greg, I'm going to try not— no, I can ask you to unmute. You should have a button on your screen if you're able to see it, and if you can't, that's okay. We'll give it a try. It is very hard on your mobile. That's okay, just give me a thumbs down if you can't find it. I do not want you to crash.
Okay, all good. We'll come back to you if you pull up. Uh, Daniel Smith, could you please, while you're eating your smoko, share with us one win from this week and something that didn't go to plan?

Uh, win would be positive conversations with team yesterday morning, um, and setting some clear boundaries and, um, bringing in the Dreamscape's way, um, of how we do work. Uh, and also sent them all their policy handbook.
Um, fabulous.

Should hopefully clear up a lot of issues we're having. And what hasn't gone our way, it's currently pissing down with rain and no one's gone out to work today. And but they're servicing all the machines, so that's also a win, I guess.

Yes, that's true. That's the weather gods have not been kind to any of us at the minute. Thank you for sharing, Daniel. I'm glad the conversations went well yesterday. Eloise, let me see if I can unmute you. I don't know why I can unmute some people and not others. I think it might be a some sort of phone setting. Hello, El.
How you going? Very well, thank you. How are you? Good, thank you.
Would you like to share a win from this week and something that didn't go to plan?
Oh goodness, I can't think of one. I've got one for you if you like. Tell me. You've paid for your trip to the Whitsundays. Oh yes, that is a win. That is a win. Yes.
We're very excited to have you. Yes. Uh, what about something that didn't go to plan then?
Something that didn't go to plan. We, oh, well, I should probably know a bit more about this, but the trades are kind of letting us down. Oh, that's frustrating.
It's a bit tough because there's not many of them and there's so many new homes coming up in town just with other builders too. Yep.
And you just can't get anyone. So very challenging, hard with workflow, but that's— yeah, anyway, it's all right, get through it.

We'll find a way. We might need to build that into your sales process a bit just so your clients are a little more understanding because you're not alone. And when everyone goes to the football club on the weekend and talks about their new they're all going to be saying the same thing.

Yeah, like, um, Ash was talking to the supervisor for the builder he used to work with. Yep. And I think they have 13 homes on the go. So that's just a little bit of an idea of trying to— all the trades, like, there's one tile or two— one tiler in town. Yep. Um, and he's gonna try and fit in everyone. And anyway, Ash is probably just going to go back to tiling everything.

So, ah, there's quite a few of our builders that do their own tiling. I think Brendan does his own, we do our own, um, Shane from Plumstruck does his own. It's quite common. So hard to get a good tiler. And once upon a time when I didn't have any painters in the community, I would have said that painters are really hard too, but now we've got a good one, he's dispelling that myth for me.
Well, there's no painters in Emerald either, so maybe we need to do a special convoy up the coast and go regional and spend a week with all the trades getting your stuff done.
Oh, crazy. Anyway, that's all right.
Well, thank you for sharing, Eloise. Appreciate it. No worries. Uh, Tara, would you like to give us a win from the week, please, and something that didn't go to plan?
A win for the week, finally got paid from the council overnight. That was delayed like 2 weeks. Um, that's just been very stressful.

Um, something I learned, I— we did some work last week which I quoted at normal business hours, and we did the work outside of business hours, and we never actually had a price adjustment for the outside hours work that we've done overnight work.
Like, all of it's been done overnight, so it should all be time and a half, double time, and I'm gonna lose out on that. Yeah, that's a shame.

So a learning curve to make sure that I get these things before— catch them beforehand. So, but in the same time, this morning I delivered a proposal, and because of the inflation of these ridiculous fuel prices because I've put an allowance in for the cost of travel to set up this said project, um, and said I'm not guaranteeing this price because I don't know what the price of fuel is. This is just an indicative price, and they were happy with that.
So great, that's wonderful. That's a big win. Well done, two big wins. Melbourne, here we come. No, good. That'll be— and Lara will need some storage.
No, I'm joking. Nice work, guys. See if you can time it around TIB90 on the 30th of April.

It's, uh, for a football activation, so it's in May. Sorry, but then we'll do something for Brisbane. Then I can come to Brisbane because it's a moving thing. But they'll let me know by the end of today if we win the tender or not. She seemed very happy, and I had put a higher price on that as well.
Wonderful. Good news. We'll see.

Thanks for sharing, guys. Keep me updated on Fox. Uh, Dylan, how about you? If you've got some good news and something that didn't go to plan— hang on, I'll have to unmute you. There you go, you're good. Um, positive, a win, um, probably the business still ticking over as I went away. First one, um, probably a negative is the way I couldn't switch my brain off while I was over there. Very common. Trust the business to run itself.
So when I'm not here, yeah, probably not enough beer then, Dylan. I know I had my, I had my quota, don't you worry. Never looking at a beer ever again. Oh no, boys trips to Vegas. Yes.

Did I say more? Don't have to say anything more. Uh, nice work, Dylan. That's a good win. And we can work on the Headspace stuff. That's really, really common. Something that we can't fix overnight, but does lessen as time goes on and you grow in confidence around the business and your team. Thank you for sharing. Um, I've covered everyone that's got a camera on.

Is there anyone that doesn't have a camera on that is able to unmute and share one win for the week and something that didn't go to plan? Just unmute and jump on in if you can. If not, we'll move on. We've had a couple of good wins. Wins.
Hello, Caleb. Hang on. Woo! Hello, Caleb. Hey, Nicole.
I can't even remember what you look like, it's been so long. It's good to have you here. Yeah, it's good to be here. Good. Tell me about your wins.

Um, so we've had a couple of good wins lately. Um, so we, after a fair bit of back and forth, um, with the customer agreeing to rates and certain things. We, we've signed up a supply contract for that certain customer.
Fabulous.

Um, so that gives us guaranteed, um, positions, um, to, to fill for those guys, uh, which is good. Um, and the other big win we've had is, yeah, we finally got a contract, um, sent to us this week for the acquisition of that other business where—
Oh, hoorah!

So there's still going to be a little bit of back and forth with that now that the— it'll go to the solicitors and they'll bounce it back and forth with each other, but we're planning on settling on that at the end of the month.
So that's fantastic, Caleb. Congratulations.
Into a, um, new workshop by early next month, I reckon.
Very good news. It never ceases to amaze me how long it takes to negotiate the purchase of a business.
Yeah, so, um, yeah, it's— that's, um, a really positive step for us, um, into the future, looking into the future. Great news, Caleb, well done.

The lot— the, the loss, which has kind of come as a bit of a, I guess you could say, um, So after locking in this contract with the customer, the guy we have had, our full-time guy, his resignation this week. So that has put us under a lot of pressure. Um, so now I will have to jump in there and, and sort of fill in for the interim until we can fill that position again.
Um, which, yeah, it comes with a lot of, a lot of stress. Yeah, certainly didn't need it at this point. No, it's a lot of overwhelm, that frustration.
I backed the car out of the driveway the other day, hit the bumper and tore the bumper off. And, uh, yeah, oh no, it wasn't a good day.

Oh no, that sounds like a very shitty day. I'm sorry, we've all been there. The worst days, it's like I don't know, I can— there's a fantastic meme on social media about when you're really, really pissed off with something and your jumper catches on the handle in the kitchen or as you walk past the door. It's kind of exactly what you're going through. I'm so sorry.
Yeah, no, but anyway, we'll, uh, I guess we'll just move on and, um, move forward.

Okay, if you've still got your ad, I'd give it a refresh and throw it back up ASAP. You can give it a refresh just by putting it into the, um, custom GPT that we did and it'll just freshen it up so it it looks a bit different, you can go straight back out to market.
Yeah, well, I've actually got an interview next lined up with the guy. Excellent.

Um, yeah, it looks pretty positive in, in that space, but, um, yeah, it's still like, you know, onboarding, all that sort of stuff. Yeah, still a good month away, so just a bit of pain in the interim.

Sorry to hear that. Business can be very painful at times. Well, I'm pleased you've got an interview lined up. Um, let's keep our fingers crossed. Smooth sailing from here for a little while for you. Yeah, yeah, yeah.
Thanks, Caleb.

Yeah, it becomes frustrating when you put a lot of time into people, like, and, um, you know, you, you, you feel like sometimes you're, you're bending over backwards to, to their needs, and then it is a slap in the face, really, like Yeah, I take it personally anyway.
So yeah, you're not alone. I think we all take it personally when we put time and effort into somebody. The key for doing this in a repeated fashion is to do it in a way that doesn't personalize it.

So it's, it's removing the— well, think of it like a Bunnings model, or think of it like a McDonald's model, the one we always talk about. If the systems are repeatable, you can put anybody into the role. And we don't have to do the heavy lifting with the training.

And then if we don't make allowances to make the role fit for them, or, you know, uh, there's another client we're working with at the moment that has made some, uh, amazing allowances for a team member, and the team member has turned around and resigned even though they had— they literally had everything they wanted.
They got a pay increase just before they resigned.
They had cut down to 4 days a week because it suited their family. They're able to have a flexible finish time so that he could pick up the children. They did all of that and he still walked away.
And so that's when I believe it becomes super personal, is you've invested so much, you've probably blurred your own boundaries. Well, they certainly had in their case.

But often for many of us, to try and make it work for someone— and we do that out of a space of fear, it's really common when we're trying to build a business and we've got someone in, we just want to hang on to them— the key is to move into a more repeated space so that we can cope with people coming and going, because they do, and make it less personal as we go.
It's not an overnight fix. It takes quite some time for the business to be steady enough for that to happen.

Typically, this happens with our first few hires. What we'd like to see is building out the resilience around that for you as a business owner so that we can do it consistently, which you will have when you take on the new business. You can have more team. It'll be there'll be less time for you to give so much of yourself to your team members. You're not alone. Many of us have made the same mistake, including myself. Thank you, Caleb. Team are frustrating.

I'm pretty sure I got a question here about team today too. Alrighty, I— what we're gonna do today, Renee's not here, so I won't talk to hers. Amy Jones has got a question, and I'd like you all to contribute. Or did anybody else who's not on camera have a win they'd like to share before we move on?
You just unmute and talk like Caleb did, if that works for you. If you can't, that's okay. Nope. Okay, Amy Jones, it's you. Would you like to unmute? Oh, your internet is not happy today.

Would you like to talk through— oh, and we've got a delay. I'm sorry if I talk over the top of you. Um, would you like to talk through your question, please? Give us all the background, because I'm going to ask everybody else to contribute.

So, um, I've just done a job ad with the TIV, um, custom GPT, um, running me through that. And one of the questions it gave me was, what are the perks of working for your company? Like, and it really got me thinking, um, what is there?

Like, um, so yeah, that that prompted the, I think it's time that we really put in some effort to create something that is worthwhile for people to leave another employer to come and work for us. Although I do believe that we're a good company to work for, we don't have any structure around the perks of working for us.

And so Brendan and I have been discussing what is it that we can actually put a structure around, be consistent with some sort of perk for working with us. And we don't want it to be around wages and money because we can't compete with the big companies there.

So we want it to be around culture and actually enjoying being at work. So, you know, whether it's a— we finish at lunchtime every Friday, whether it's a a pub lunch for the guys every second Friday? Is it a monthly or quarterly team-building activity like go-karts or like something kind of fun?

Only having a small team, like sometimes there's only Brendan and one labourer and me, kind of doing like a barbecue on a Friday afternoon feels silly because there's only 3 people. Like, it doesn't feel comfortable for me.

I don't enjoy it. So, yeah, just wondering, yeah, if anybody else had any really good ideas that we can look at. But we really want to actually structure it and have it built into a calendar and follow through.
I'd have some ideas for you, but before I throw to everybody else, I want to well, give some words of caution.

Just like Caleb has just outlined, the problem with creating the perks, if you like— I'll use your language— is that we as employers are giving, giving, giving to our team in the hope of building that culture within the team. So I'll take it back to kids, right?
Most of us parents, Giving to our kids never makes better behaved kids. What makes better behaved kids that are engaged, want to be part of the family, is having boundaries in place.

And so part of, you know, where Caleb is feeling really frustrated, and he's quite right to, because he's gone over and above the ordinary to create, um, uh, an environment somebody wants to come to work in.
Part of that frustration is we have given so much of ourselves we've put so much effort in to try and make it fantastic for someone and they can't see the value.

Because the truth of it is most people don't go to work for culture, they don't stick around for culture, they stick around for money, or they stick around for something else that works for them. That's kind of where I think you're going with the perks, or the idea of the perks.

So I guess when we're talking broadly, for any of you on the call, I'd hate for you all to rush off and create these amazing tailored solutions for your team members that don't necessarily serve your business. Everything should be attached to an outcome for your business.
So I just wanted to have that, that side of caution. It's the firm boundaries, it's the consistency, and it's the trust with you as a business owner that creates good employees and a strong culture.

That— we'll actually be working on this, so you'll see in this week's newsletter on Monday that the first mini sprints day that we're ever doing, and the first one for this year, will be on the 30th of April in Melbourne.
It will be a hybrid event, so you'll be able to jump on online just like we do with our TIB90s, and we will be building out your business meeting structure.

And a large part of that, in fact most of them, is a HR structure that if you follow and you follow tightly, you will produce fantastic team members very consistently for you. Part of that, however, means that team members move on.

So team members that don't fit naturally move on because of the way we manage this system. And that's actually the outcome that we want, even though generally we're hiring, we're quite fearful about hiring, it's a big investment to hire, and we get frightened about people moving on.
But we want them to move on before they become a problem. We don't want to have them when they're not performing the way we want them to.

So largely, if I come back to this, Amy, I would like all of these to be rewards for a level of performance rather than a standard that's built into the business that everybody gets regardless. It's almost like a performance bonus, if you like. So that might come around your KPIs.
It might be that they've met their KPIs for the quarter, and so accordingly they get this, and this, as you say, might be some kind of social activity.
Um, I'll speak for Liam and Tamara because they're not here.

One of the things that is important for them, and I do think this one in particular is a really good idea, I think it's 4 days— I'd have to check with them— 4 days in a year they give their team members that they tack on to existing long weekends so that their team members get a bigger rest 4 times a year, uh, to help with their productivity.

So you think— I think that would actually be really helpful for you, Amy, because you're— anybody that's working with you guys, the physicality is extreme. Whether you're a laborer and you're working with very heavy stuff, or you're a climber and you're climbing very high trees, it doesn't matter.

Either way, it's extremely heavy work that your people are doing. So the benefit and the reason why Liam and Samara have done this as builders is their carpenters are also doing very hot and heavy work, or very cold and heavy work.
And so it means that it gives their team an opportunity for more rest, so they're more productive when they come back. They've spaced that out.

I'm pretty sure it's one a quarter, so they might have— it's already a 3-day long weekend. Let's say they get the Monday off, a public holiday, they give them the Friday off as well. So I think that's a great idea for anybody with real physical jobs to adopt.

Um, rescately, in your case, probably wouldn't because they get sufficient downtime anyway. But as you build out and it's more consistent, then I think that would be a great way. To reward your team. So that's an area I think that you could build in that builds in some culture.
We like you to take your rest and downtime.

You're showing respect to them without really moving boundaries, because the boundary still here is that you want performance from them in exchange for that. Okay, what I'd like you all to do is take some time. Those of you that have something you'd like to contribute, unmute, share.

I'll help manage if you all talk at once. I'll pick one of you to talk. But I'd like this to be a little bit more collaborative because we're only getting a couple of questions each week at the moment. This is going to help us fill out the time with some other suggestions.
So has anybody got some suggestions they'd like to share with Amy? Tara's got a few suggestions in the chat.

She's saying, can you give them their birthday off? Brad suggested if a team member has been there for maybe 4 years, They are entitled to an extra few days of leave a year. Again, that's putting a benchmark in, in place for people to follow. Anybody else got suggestions, things they've done or seen that they like?
Even something you've seen didn't work. I had one of our business owners 2 weeks ago had a golf day for their team.

2 of the team resigned the day before and still got to the golf day, got to go to the golf day, and somebody else major— made a major area error the day before and they still got to go to the, the golf day. I can't see that's a reward. That just becomes an expectation for the team.
Uh, Dylan takes the team out for their birth— on their birthday for lunch.

I think that's a great idea. I think you can do some surprise and delights, Amy, that doesn't actually get built in in a way that you could market it. So you could market the extra days off because we value their downtime. I think surprise and delight carries a lot more weight than anything that's expected.

Expectations create burdens for business owners. Surprise and delight might be, we got through the job quicker this week. You can have Friday off with pay. Or surprise and delight might be, oh, we're finished early, let's go out for lunch.
They're just occasional things that we do that make people feel good. Um, which is stuff that we do anyway, I think. Exactly.
Um, but yeah, I think it was just when I was doing that, um, GPT for the ad, it was like a specific question like, what, what do you offer?.

And I'm like, oh, like there's nothing that I can say we do this consistently other than, you know, if we've had a really good week, like the guys, you know, we might sit in a bit of cash and go, thanks for, you know, you've worked really hard this week. Thanks for, for doing that.

Um, yeah, I do like the extra, the extra days tacked onto the long weekends because that actually works in with, with our lifestyle as well because we like to go camping every long weekend and we would generally take an extra day anyway. Way. So maybe to actually work that in as an extra for the team.

Yep, I agree. And I think what Tara's just highlighted is also a perk— your support. Once upon a time, we used flexibility a lot. I think the workforce is changing. Flexibility has become an expectation because of some new employment laws.

So I don't know that mentioning that is necessarily a positive, but I do think that you're a very supportive environment. You have, um, you talk about the team culture that you've had previously. I think that you could talk to the benefit of education, the experience, how long you guys have been doing what you do.

All of that's actually quite attractive to the right applicant rather than talking about, you know, perks is a pretty broad word for what separates you from everyone else. Part of what separates you is you're a small team, which allows more time and attention for training and support.

Yeah. So maybe I've just overthought the entire thing. And, yeah, we actually are doing little— on hot days, I'll take ice blocks to the boys, or I'll just randomly turn up on site with breakfast or lunch and stuff. So we are doing it. Yeah, it was just that I feel I couldn't write anything without having evidence as like it's consistent and it's structured.

So, yeah. I don't think it needs to be that defined. I also think that the professionalism, like you think about the other mobs in your area, none of them are really offering careers, are they? And they're not really offering any level of professionalism. They're offering a job. So that could be something you talk to as well.

Yeah, which is what we would generally do, um, point out that our team are team members, they're not numbers. They're, you know, we actually care about everybody and everybody kind of like respects everybody. So, okay, yeah, no, I've just overthought it and maybe just need to adjust the wording so that I feel too forwardy, um, with making big statements in an ad.

Yep, perfect. Thank you, Amy. Thank you for your suggestions, everybody. You're all very shy today. Um, Okay, I want to ask a broad question for you all, and I'd like you to give me a show of hands, or if you can't give me a show of hands, drop it into the chat for me.

Who, when first focusing on their numbers in the business, found it really uncomfortable? Give me a show of hands if you did. Yep, or drop it into the chat for me. I just wanted everybody to feel normal for a moment.

Still do. Yep, most of us do. I hate talking about money. I don't like navigating those conversations. I don't even like when I have to talk to you guys about dashboards. Uh, it's everybody's biggest struggle, Dylan. It's not— none— I don't know a single tradie, um, that went into business because they liked working on spreadsheets. They might like money.

I think all of us like money, but we don't like looking at money. We don't like talking about money. We don't like feeling those feelings of being inadequate or not having enough or not knowing or feeling that vulnerability around thinking we don't know enough.
It's why I push through my own discomfort to talk to you all about it, because I don't think it needs to be complicated.

I think part of the problem is we live in a world with lots of very intelligent accountant-type people who speak their entire own language about a position that we have in business that actually doesn't matter to us much because it doesn't impact the bank account.

They're talking about a tax position, something that we don't need to think quite so much about. For us, on an everyday basis, we need money in the bank to pay bills, and it should be as simple as that.

And I think when we take away some of the complex language that goes around, um, money and how to figure out our position, when we make it really basic, suddenly it makes much more sense and you grow in confidence.
